Abstract

We analyze the performance of different sen- timent classification models on syntactically- complex inputs like A-but-B sentences. The first contribution of this analysis addresses re- producible research: to meaningfully compare different models, their accuracies must be av- eraged over far more random seeds than what has traditionally been reported.

afaaab64-59aa-4880-8604-e3dc6a51d297.label: text_chunk
afaaab64-59aa-4880-8604-e3dc6a51d297.text:
With proper averaging in place, we notice that the distil- lation model described in Hu et al.

 ( 2016 ), which incorporates explicit logic rules for sen- timent classification, is ineffective. In contrast, using contextualized ELMo embeddings ( Pe- ters et al. , 2018a ) instead of logic rules yields significantly better performance. Additionally, we provide analysis and visualizations that demonstrate ELMo’s ability to implicitly learn logic rules.

159917d6-e3f2-44c0-a31d-a9dd9932c808.label: text_chunk
159917d6-e3f2-44c0-a31d-a9dd9932c808.text:
Finally, a crowdsourced analysis reveals how ELMo outperforms baseline mod- els even on sentences with ambiguous senti- ment labels.

1 Introduction

In this paper, we explore the effectiveness of meth- ods designed to improve sentiment classification (positive vs. negative) of sentences that con- tain complex syntactic structures.

b3a802ac-6aeb-4faf-a660-b4aefacc64f0.label: text_chunk
b3a802ac-6aeb-4faf-a660-b4aefacc64f0.text:
While simple bag-of-words or lexicon-based methods ( Pang and Lee , 2005 ; Wang and Manning , 2012 ; Iyyer et al.

 , 2015 ) achieve good performance on this task, they are unequipped to deal with syntactic structures that affect sentiment, such as contrastive conjunc- tions (i.e., sentences of the form “A-but-B” ) or negations.

290fafe4-76e1-49cb-8417-eb0fdc81c00f.label: text_chunk
290fafe4-76e1-49cb-8417-eb0fdc81c00f.text:
Neural models that explicitly encode word order ( Kim , 2014 ), syntax ( Socher et al. , 2013 ; Tai et al. , 2015 ) and semantic features ( Li et al.

 , 2017 ) have been proposed with the aim of improving performance on these more compli- cated sentences. Recently, Hu et al.

4319e9fe-2175-4dee-a4cd-2086008b1ef9.label: text_chunk
4319e9fe-2175-4dee-a4cd-2086008b1ef9.text:
( 2016 ) in- corporate logical rules into a neural model and
show that these rules increase the model’s accu- racy on sentences containing contrastive conjunc- tions, while Peters et al.

 ( 2018a ) demonstrate in- creased overall accuracy on sentiment analysis by initializing a model with representations from a language model trained on millions of sentences.In this work, we carry out an in-depth study of the effectiveness of the techniques in Hu et al.

9023cc67-6aa1-48d6-9b0f-d6f92a739a43.label: text_chunk
9023cc67-6aa1-48d6-9b0f-d6f92a739a43.text:
( 2016 ) and Peters et al. ( 2018a ) for sentiment clas- sification of complex sentences.

 Part of our con- tribution is to identify an important gap in the methodology used in Hu et al. ( 2016 ) for perfor- mance measurement, which is addressed by av- eraging the experiments over several executions.

00cc99a9-2921-4e3b-8d57-f2b474f3d88a.label: text_chunk
00cc99a9-2921-4e3b-8d57-f2b474f3d88a.text:
With the averaging in place, we obtain three key findings: (1) the improvements in Hu et al.

 ( 2016 ) can almost entirely be attributed to just one of their two proposed mechanisms and are also less pronounced than previously reported; (2) contex- tualized word embeddings ( Peters et al.

38b9c6d8-511e-4afa-8e07-8975a609c5d5.label: text_chunk
38b9c6d8-511e-4afa-8e07-8975a609c5d5.text:
, 2018a ) incorporate the “A-but-B” rules more effectively without explicitly programming for them; and (3) an analysis using crowdsourcing reveals a big- ger picture where the errors in the automated sys- tems have a striking correlation with the inherent sentiment-ambiguity in the data.

2 Logic Rules in Sentiment Classiﬁcation

Here we briefly review background from Hu et al.

696d6244-8ad7-4489-a611-3ee045e0486c.label: text_chunk
696d6244-8ad7-4489-a611-3ee045e0486c.text:
( 2016 ) to provide a foundation for our reanalysis in the next section. We focus on a logic rule for sentences containing an “A-but-B” structure (the only rule for which Hu et al. ( 2016 ) provide exper- imental results).

 Intuitively, the logic rule for such sentences is that the sentiment associated with the whole sentence should be the same as the senti- ment associated with phrase “B” .

3 A Reanalysis

In this section we reanalyze the effectiveness of the techniques of Hu et al. ( 2016 ) and find that most of the performance gain is due to projection and not knowledge distillation.

4 Contextualized Word Embeddings

Traditional context-independent word embed- dings like word2vec ( Mikolov et al. , 2013 ) or GloVe ( Pennington et al. , 2014 ) are fixed vec- tors for every word in the vocabulary.

The relationships surrounding the ELMo model highlight its development, functionality, and performance in natural language processing tasks. 

1. **Development and Foundation**: ELMo was developed by Peters et al., building upon the foundational work of Kim (2014), which introduced contextualized embeddings to enhance word meaning understanding.

2. **Integration with Other Models**: ELMo embeddings are utilized to enhance the performance of CNN models in sentiment analysis. Specifically, ELMo fine-tunes both LSTM and CNN weights during training, indicating its integral role in model optimization.

3. **Performance Evaluation**: ELMo is evaluated on the SST2 dataset for sentiment classification, where it demonstrates superior performance, particularly on A-but-B style sentences. Comparisons are made between ELMo and baseline models, showcasing its effectiveness in processing non-neutral sentences.

4. **Results Presentation**: The performance improvements achieved through ELMo embeddings are documented in Table 2, while Figure 3 illustrates findings related to fine-tuned ELMo embeddings, contributing to the broader discussion on embeddings.

5. **Comparative Analysis**: ELMo's predictions are assessed against the A-but-B rule, providing insights into its alignment with specific linguistic structures. However, it is noted that distillation does not yield significant improvements compared to ELMo's performance.

6. **Citations and References**: The significance of ELMo is further underscored by its citation in Peters et al. (2018b), indicating its relevance in ongoing research discussions.

Overall, ELMo represents a significant advancement in the field of natural language processing, demonstrating its utility in enhancing model performance and its foundational role in subsequent research.
